issues.title: (AD Review) Normative Language Check through the document
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
The document includes many SHOULD/SHOULD NOT/RECOMMENDED that don't adhere to the guidance at: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/statement-iesg-statement-on-clarifying-the-use-of-bcp-14-key-words/ (search for "On the use of “SHOULD” and “RECOMMENDED”", in particular).

Please review all occurences and provide an explanation of the effects of not following a reco among the lines:

"“SHOULD do X because if you don’t, you get Y, which is less preferable because Z”."

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body:
I'm not sure. The document's recommendations are, after all, recommendations, and SHOULD (= RECOMMENDED) appears very appropriate for that. The IESG statement says that it "improves the document to provide readers with all of the details they need to make an informed decision".

In fact, all the details are provided in the analysis / rationale sections, so readers can actually make a very informed deviating decision based on what's in the document. This particularly applies to registries, who have quite some leeway regarding their local policies.

The recommendation context just isn't in the same sentence as each SHOULD, but that is not required and would also make the document significantly harder to read.

It also seemed to be the view of DNSOP participants, including (earlier) ADs (Warren, Paul W) that this is a fine use of the keyword. I'd thus suggest considering this addressed.

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
> It also seemed to be the view of DNSOP participants, including (earlier) ADs (Warren, Paul W) that this is a fine use of the keyword. I'd thus suggest considering this addressed.

I'm afraid this will be problematic as there are many blocking DISCUSSes these days on this specific point.

I see two options:

* Either have one statement early in the document to explain the reasoning and that for all SHOULD/RECO: not following the reco will lead to ambiguity, interop issues, etc. Make that statement clear right afte the blob about normative language.
 
* Handle it one by one

Please pick one option. Thanks.

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body:
> have one statement early in the document to explain the reasoning and that for all SHOULD/RECO: not following the reco will lead to ambiguity, interop issues, etc. Make that statement clear right afte the blob about normative language.

Will do.

issues.title: (AD Review) Embedded reco in the analysis part
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
There are several recommendations that are provided in the discussion part, while I was expecting those to be part of the guidance.

Not sure how to interpret those.

Should those be moved oustide of the analysis section? Avoid using noramtive language in the analysis section?

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body:
Those normative statements in the analysis sections are not additional ones, but flow directly from the actual recommendations in the rec sections.

For example, when the recommendation says you SHOULD be doing some check, then the rationale says "this thing may happen and preventing it is a good thing, so you SHOULD be doing this check; you MAY of course do other things in addition but in any case you MUST NOT proceed if the situation doesn't make sense". Those are not meant as additional requirements. I'd be completely fine lowercasing them (to simply make them part of the argument flow), except that I can foresee COMMENTs that this keywords have to be uppercase (which I disagree with, because not every use of the word "may" necessarily is normative, for example). -- Would you be ok with that?

As these instances are really part of the rationale, I'd strongly prefer to not move them elsewhere.

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body: The requirements notation actually says that keywords should be interpreted normatively "when, and only when, they appear in all capitals" -- in other words, lowercasing them is admissible and also meaningful (just not normative). So I think we should do that. Agree?

comments.author: boucadair
comments.body:
The initial concern I had is that if one "consumes" the set of reco listed in "Recommendations Overview", it won't be exposed the detailed items under analsysis (that are not mirrored in the main reco).

> The requirements notation actually says that keywords should be interpreted normatively "when, and only when, they appear in all capitals" -- in other words, lowercasing them is admissible and also meaningful (just not normative). So I think we should do that. Agree?

Following this in the analysis sections works for me.

issues.title: (IESTF Last Call)  Meir Goldman Review
issues.author: boucadair
issues.body:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/last-call/6Hx3iqm3LoWdfh4QOv1OvmAGHbc/:


> "I support the direction of draft-ietf-dnsop-ds-automation. One operational gap worth tightening is audit continuity for automated DS decisions.
> The draft already recommends acceptance checks, cancellation when checks fail, notifications, and optional exposure of DS update history through customer portals. For operational safety, I suggest adding a short recommendation that parental agents retain structured decision records for automated DS maintenance, including failed or cancelled requests.
> 
> Suggested addition to Section 5.1 or Section 9:
> 
> Entities performing automated DS maintenance SHOULD retain structured records of DS automation decisions for a locally defined retention period. Records SHOULD include the child zone, triggering CDS/CDNSKEY RRsets or notification channel, authoritative nameserver set consulted, verification results, decision outcome, applied DS RRset or cancellation reason, timestamp, and the responsible parental agent. These records are intended to support troubleshooting, dispute resolution, and post-incident analysis, and need not be made publicly available.
> 
> Rationale:
> 
> Automated DS processing can affect DNSSEC validation and domain reachability. Operators need a reliable way to reconstruct why an automated DS change was accepted, rejected, cancelled, or rolled back. This also helps distinguish child-side publication problems, parental-agent acceptance-check failures, concurrent update conflicts, and operational errors."
>

pulls.title: Replace "owner" with "holder"
pulls.author: oskar456
pulls.body: It is tricky to talk about ownership of a domain name. The term holder is better suited here.

pulls.title: First AD Review round
pulls.author: boucadair
pulls.body:
* Further insist this is a BCP with OPS focus
* Build on existing docs for the terminology
* Flow check
* edits to enhance readability
* Normative reference checks

reviews.author: peterthomassen
reviews.body:
I've corrected a few typos.

"Parental Agent" is actually the only term imported from RFC 7344; the others mentions from that RFC were redundant.

Your changes exposed "new deployments" in a section title. This was not the case previously, and looks like the recommendations don't apply to existing ones. Of course, they do; it's just that immediate implementation is not required while new deployments should be starting out as described. Anyway, recs apply to either, so I've removed "new". It technically makes no differences but reads much better.

reviews.author: boucadair
reviews.body: Your fixes look good to me. Thanks Peter.

pulls.title: 20260415 ad review
pulls.author: peterthomassen
pulls.body: @boucadair tried to assign you for review but apparently that requires adding you to the team first (sent you an invite)

reviews.author: boucadair
reviews.body: Looks good, except one check on noramative ref.

comments.body: Now that we don't use the normative language MAY, please check that we still need {{!RFC5730}}/!RFC9803 to be Normative.

Good catch! 4 brain hemispheres see more than 2 :)

Fixed, will squash, push, merge. Thanks.

pulls.title: nit: align the reco appendix with the reco in main body
pulls.author: boucadair

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body: @boucadair I am not sure whether my reply in the inline conversation caused a notification, so allow me to ping you again :)

comments.author: peterthomassen
comments.body: Thanks. The force-push was only for squashing; no changes in HEAD content.

```suggestion
When implementing these recommendations, operators have to carefully weigh whether any particular deviation is justified in their particular context.
```

Applied your change.

Also, added another commit in response to a clarification request from Oli Schacher (Switch/.CH, encountered during implementation in March).

```suggestion
If both RRsets are published, Parents are expected to verify consistency between them by verifying that they refer to the same set of keys {{!I-D.ietf-dnsop-cds-consistency}}. The CDS digest field needs only be verified when the hash digest algorithm is designated as "MUST" in the "Implement for DNSSEC Delegation" column of the "Domain Name System Security (DNSSEC) Algorithm Numbers" registry group {{DS-IANA}}, and may otherwise be ignored when the digest type is unsupported.
```

"need" does not need an "s" here, see https://painintheenglish.com/case/4148

As we're referring to a column, we should name the registry, not the registry group. The registry is just called "Digest Algorithms"; I've decided to keep it at that for readability and not name the overarching group. The reference makes it clear anyway.

Pls let me know if this works for your and I'll merge. thx!
